questions.scenario: A release note says `orders-api v31 deployed`, but it does not name the slot or revision serving users. What is missing?

options.text: Runtime target evidence that identifies where the artifact is actually running.
A screenshot of the pipeline success page.

questions.explanation: A version label is not enough. The team needs the exact slot, revision, resource, and traffic target.

questions.scenario: The team is comparing staging and production behavior after a release. Which runtime facts can explain why the same artifact behaves differently?

options.text: Different app settings or secret references.
Different managed identities or role assignments.
Different traffic split or slot exposure.
The same commit SHA in both environments.

questions.explanation: The same commit can still behave differently when runtime state differs.

questions.scenario: A health check passes in a candidate slot, but checkout fails after swap. What is the best conclusion?

options.text: The health check proved one layer, but the release still needs user-path and dependency evidence.
The health check result must have been false and should be deleted.
The failure cannot be release-related because health passed.
The team should roll back without checking dependency or config evidence.

questions.explanation: Health checks are useful gates, but they do not prove every business path works.
